title: Naree
description: A feminist Bengali work challenging patriarchy, religious orthodoxy, and gender hierarchy.
descriptionParagraphs: A feminist Bengali work challenging patriarchy, religious orthodoxy, and gender hierarchy.

Naree is usually read through its treatment of feminism, religion, and gender politics. As a non-fiction, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes feminism, religion, and gender politics feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A feminist Bengali work challenging patriarchy, religious orthodoxy, and gender hierarchy.
banningParagraphs: Naree entered censorship debates as a non-fiction associated with feminism, religion, and gender politics. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around religious offense and gender politics.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1995-2000 in Bangladesh, where Government of Bangladesh banned circulation. Authorities targeted the book after pressure from conservative religious groups. It is an important example of a feminist text banned in the name of religious order.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
